name: fspathtree

metadata.name: fspathtree
metadata.summary: A small utility for wrapping trees (nested dict/list) that allows filesystem-like path access, including walking up with "../".

documentation:
# FS Path Tree

A simple class to allow filesystem-style path access to nested dict/list structures,
including support for walking "up" the tree with '..'.

Example:

```python
  config = fspathtree()
  config.update( { 'desc' : "example config"
                 , 'time' : { 'N' : 50
                            , 'dt' : 0.01 }
                 , 'grid' : { 'x' : { 'min' : 0
                                    , 'max' : 0.5
                                    , 'N' : 100 }
                            , 'y' : { 'min' : 1
                                    , 'max' : 1.5
                                    , 'N' : 200 }
                            }
                 } )

  # elements are accessed in the same was as a dict.
  assert config['desc'] == "example config"
  # sub-elements can also be accessed the same way.
  assert config['grid']['x']['max'] == 0.5
  # but they can also be accessed using a path.
  assert config['grid/x/max'] == 0.5

  # get a sub-element in the tree.
  x = config['grid/x']

  # again, elements of grid/x are accessed as normal.
  assert x['max'] == 0.5
  # but we can also access elements that are not in this branch.
  assert x['../y/max'] == 1.5
  # or reference elements from the root of the tree.
  assert x['/time/N'] == 50
```

## API

The `fspathtree` class can be default constructed, with a `dict`, or with another `fspathtree`
```python
t1 = fspathtree()
t2 = fspathtree({'one':1})
t3 = fspathtree(t2)
```

Elements in the tree can be tested for presence using the `in` operator. They can be accessed with the
`__call__` operator or the `get(...)` method.
```
t = fspathtree({'one':{'two': 2}})

assert '/one/two' in t
assert t['/one/two'] == 2
assert '/one/three' not in t

t['/one/three'] = 3
assert '/one/three' in t
assert t['/one/three'] == 3

assert t.get('/one/three', None) == 3
assert t.get('/one/missing', None) is None
```
The `get_all_paths()` method returns a list of all paths in the tree (it actually returns a generator).
`get_all_leaf_node_paths()` returns a list of only leaf node paths. Both accept a predicate that can
be used to filer the paths that are returned.
```python
t = fspathtree({'one':{'two': 2}})

paths = list( t.get_all_paths() ) # returns [PathType('/'), PathType('/one'), PathType('two')]
paths = list( t.get_all_leafe_node_paths() ) # returns [PathType('two')]
paths = list( t.get_all_paths(predicate p: p.name == 'one') ) # returns [ PathType('/one')]

```


## Install

You can install the latest release with `pip`.
```
$ pip install fspathtree
```
Or, even better, using `pipenv`
```
$ pipenv install fspathtree
```
Or, even better better, using `poetry`
```
$ poetry add fspathtree
```

## Design

The `fspathtree` is a small wrapper class that can wrap any nested tree data structure. The tree that is wrapped can be accessed with
the `.tree` attribute. This is an improvement over the old `fspathdict.pdict` class, which stored nodes internally as `fspathdict.pdict` instances
and required "converting" to and from the standard python dict and list types.

classes.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.is_leaf
documentation: Return true if node is a leaf. i.e. not a mapping or list.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.__getitem__
documentation:
Return node located at `path`.

`path` is a '/' separated list of keys/indices to the node.
Public Instance API

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.__setitem__
documentation:
Set the element located at `path` to `value`.

The `path` is treated as a filesystem-like path, with parts separated by '/' characters.
If the path represented by `path` includes "parents" that do not exists, they will be created.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.update
documentation:
Update tree with entries from another fspathtree and/or dict/list.

Note: if an fspathtree is passed as an argument, then the tree is updated recursively. If a dict is passed,
then the standard dict.update(...) method is used. This means that tree branches will be overwritten if
passing a plain dict.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.path
documentation:
Return absolute path to the current node.

Note that leaf notes are returned as thier value and lose knowlege of being part
of a "tree". For example, this does not work

    tree['/one/two/three'] = 3
    assert tree['/one/two/three'].path() == '/one/two/three'

because the call to tree['/one/two/three'] returns the integer 3.
i.e. it does not return a proxy type that keeps track of the tree.

If you want tree values that 'know' about their location in the
tree, use a special key name for values

    tree['/one/two/three/@value'] = 3
    assert tree['/one/two/three'].path() == '/one/two/three'
    assert tree['/one/two/three'][@value] == 3

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.get
documentation: Returns the value of the node references by path, or a default value if the node does not exist.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._instance_get_all_paths
documentation: this is used to allow the same name for instance and static methods

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.normalize_path
documentation:
Return a normalized version of given path.
Normalization removes all '..' and '.' elements.

/one/two/three/../../four  -> /one/four
Public Static API

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.getitem
documentation:
Given a tree and a path, returns the value of the node pointed to by the path. The path will be normalized first.

path may be specified as a string, Path-like object, or list of path elements.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.setitem
documentation:
Given a tree, a path, and a value, sets the value of the node pointed to by the path. If any level of the path does not
exist, it is created. If the _root_ node type needs to be changed to set the value, then a new tree with the value set
is returned.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.get_all_leaf_node_paths
documentation:
Return a list of all paths that point leaf notes (i.e. point to values)

`transform` is an optional callable that can be used to transform the paths
before returning. This can be used to remove leading '/' for example.
Each leaf node path will be passed to `transform(path)` and the return value
will be returned.

`predicate` is an optional callable that can be used to determine if the path
should be returned. This is useful for filting leaf node paths.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree.find
documentation: Return all leaf node paths matching `pattern`.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._make_path
documentation: Given a string, bytes array, integer, or list of path elements;  return a PathType object representing the path.
Private Methods

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._normalize_path_parts
documentation:
Normalize a list of path parts. i.e. remove ".." and "." elements
while maintaining the same path.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._getitem_from_path_parts
documentation:
@param tree           A nested Mapping/Sequence (i.e. dict/list) object that is being accessed.
@param path_parts     A list of path elements identifying the location of the element being accessed.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._setitem_from_path_parts
fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._get_all_paths
documentation: Return a list of all paths in the tree.

methods.name: fspathtree.fspathtree.fspathtree._get_all_leaf_node_paths
documentation: Returns a list containing the paths to all leaf nodes in the tree.
A small class that wraps a tree data struction and allow accessing the nested elements using filesystem-style paths.
